channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Muscle mitochondria, function, mass, and quality of life in prostate cancer during androgen deprivation therapy
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73542-x

Prostate cancer and androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) impair muscle function and quality of life, yet mitochondrial involvement remains unclear. The authors show that greater mitochondrial function correlates with better endurance, quality of life, and smaller declines in these parameters post-ADT
item.dc:title: Muscle mitochondria, function, mass, and quality of life in prostate cancer during androgen deprivation therapy
item.dc:creator: L. Caeiro
L. J. Anderson
A. Dash
D. J. Marcinek
H. L. Kerr
L. Paulsen

S. A. Gharib
J. M. Garcia

item.title: Very low-carbohydrate ketogenic diet in treatment-naïve women with endometrial cancer and overweight: a randomized feasibility study
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73519-w

Dietary interventions have been proposed as potential therapeutic strategies in cancer. Here, the authors report a feasibility randomized clinical trial comparing standard diet with a very lowcarbohydrate ketogenic diet (VLCD) in treatment-naive women with endometrial cancer and obesity or overweight.
item.dc:title: Very low-carbohydrate ketogenic diet in treatment-naïve women with endometrial cancer and overweight: a randomized feasibility study

item.title: Monitoring in vivo transcription with synthetic serum markers
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73486-2

Typically, transcription in vivo is measured post-mortem or in biopsied tissue samples. Here, the authors show a method where transcript levels can be reported in living animals with synthetic serum markers and simple blood test.
item.dc:title: Monitoring in vivo transcription with synthetic serum markers
item.dc:creator: Sho Watanabe
Sangsin Lee
Manwal Harb
Shirin Nouraein
Emma Raisley
Honghao Li
Nicolas Buitrago
Beatrice Pforr
Jerzy O. Szablowski

item.title: Explainable time-series forecasting with sampling-free SHAP for Transformers
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73243-5

Time-series forecasting is crucial for decision making in many domains. The authors propose SHAPformer, a method for accurate and explainable time-series forecasting, delivering explanations in less than a second. It is validated on synthetic data and used to gain insights into electrical load and electricity price data.
item.dc:title: Explainable time-series forecasting with sampling-free SHAP for Transformers
item.dc:creator: Matthias Hertel
Sebastian Pütz
Ralf Mikut
Veit Hagenmeyer
Benjamin Schäfer

item.title: Deciphering cytokine-driven ADP-ribosylation signaling networks via Af1521-based mass spectrometry analysis of labile Glu/Asp-linkages
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73677-x

Labile ADP-ribosylation has remained challenging to study. Here, the authors develop a mass spectrometry workflow that enables mapping of these modifications and reveals roles in immune and antiviral signaling.
item.dc:title: Deciphering cytokine-driven ADP-ribosylation signaling networks via Af1521-based mass spectrometry analysis of labile Glu/Asp-linkages
item.dc:creator: Sara C. Buch-Larsen
Ivo A. Hendriks
Kyuto Tashiro
Jonas D. Elsborg
Sergey Y. Vakhrushev
Jesper V. Olsen
Bernhard Lüscher
Glen Liszczak
Ivan Ahel
Michael L. Nielsen

item.title: Telomere-to-telomere genome assembly of Oryza australiensis reveals transposon-driven centromere repositioning and shared EE–DD ancestry
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73769-8

Oryza australiensis (EE genome) is a wild rice species with extreme environmental adaptability and grain properties. Here, the authors report its telomere-to-telomere genome assembly and clarify that the ancestral lineage that contributed the DD subgenome shared common ancestry with an EE-like progenitor.
item.dc:title: Telomere-to-telomere genome assembly of Oryza australiensis reveals transposon-driven centromere repositioning and shared EE–DD ancestry
item.dc:creator: Yu Bao
Hanli You
Shuo Liu
Guanqing Liu
Yuechao Wu
Qingqing Yang
Qi You
Peng Liu
Chuandeng Yi
Wenli Zhang
Zhukuan Cheng
Tao Zhang

item.title: Functional modules for enhanced amorphous composite halide solid electrolytes for low-temperature all-solid-state lithium batteries
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71876-0

Designing improved halide solid electrolytes holds the key to the success of all-solid-state batteries. Here, authors present the design of SSEs through functional modules, introducing LaCl3, which possesses a UCl3 structure, as well as low-cost and low-density AlF3, to enable low-temperature application and improve moisture and high-voltage stability in halide-based solid-state battery systems.
item.dc:title: Functional modules for enhanced amorphous composite halide solid electrolytes for low-temperature all-solid-state lithium batteries
item.dc:creator: Yanlong Wu
Xinmiao Wang

item.title: Highly dynamic assemblies of glycolytic enzymes by quinary determinants
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-73795-6

Molecular channelling in glycolysis is debated. Here, authors show short-lived enzyme assemblies, named protein flocks, in yeast and human cells. Shared surface properties suggest quinary interactions boost efficiency and limit toxic intermediates.
item.dc:title: Highly dynamic assemblies of glycolytic enzymes by quinary determinants
item.dc:creator: Blanca Poquet
Galal Yahya
Laia Ferrer
Enrique Marcos
Carme Gallego
Martí Aldea
